title: The Rise of Christian Nationalism
source: The Guardian - The Nation – July - August 2026
content:
In recent years, several political leaders have famously ordered public schools to incorporate the Bible and the Ten Commandments into their standard educational curriculum. Multiple states have also approved conservative-media educational materials for classrooms, which often present highly controversial historical interpretations. However, critics point out that these state-sponsored curricula rarely focus on the actual moral teachings found in religious texts. Instead of emphasizing commands to feed the poor, care for the sick, or welcome the stranger, these mandates focus on right-wing authoritarian political indoctrination. To modern Christian nationalists, any argument that America was never intended to be a Christian nation sounds like absolute heresy.

Interestingly, while the overall percentage of Americans identifying as religious continues to steadily decline, the percentage of Christians who believe America was founded as a strictly Christian nation is paradoxically increasing. In other words, as the broader religious population shrinks, the most extreme ideological fringe is rapidly growing. This movement frequently ignores that historical political power in early America was not intended to be in a deeply codependent marriage with religious institutions. Many founding documents explicitly state that the nation was not founded on adherence to some particular religious creed. Ultimately, the ongoing push to merge church and state threatens the fundamental secular principles that the country's founders originally wrote down.
